publications.title: Germ line-governed recognition of a cancer epitope by an immunodominant human T-cell receptor.
publications.authors: Cole DK, Yuan F, Rizkallah PJ, Miles JJ, Gostick E, Price DA, Gao GF, Jakobsen BK, Sewell AK.
publications.executive_summary: A TRAV12-2-encoded TCR specific for the MART-1-derived melanoma epitope ELAGIGILTV (ELA) presented by HLA-A*0201 was structurally characterized to explain why this TCR gene segment is used in >87% of CD8+ T cells targeting this dominant tumor antigen. The structure reveals that germ line-encoded regions of the TCR alpha chain dominate contacts with the peptide, an unusual "innate" pattern of antigen recognition that contrasts with typical CDR3-mediated engagement. This germ line-driven recognition mechanism accounts for the exceptional frequency and prevalence of TRAV12-2-based responses to MART-1 in the naive repertoire, with implications for understanding natural anti-tumor immunity and potentially cancer vaccine and TCR engineering strategies targeting this immunodominant epitope.

publications.title: Structural basis for ineffective T-cell responses to MHC anchor residue-improved "heteroclitic" peptides.
publications.authors: Madura F, Rizkallah PJ, Holland CJ, Fuller A, Bulek A, Godkin AJ, Schauenburg AJ, Cole DK, Sewell AK.
publications.executive_summary: This study examined the TCR recognition of wild-type and engineered variants of the MART-1/Melan-A peptide (EAAGIGILTV and its leucine-2 heteroclitic variant ELAGIGILTV) bound to HLA-A*0201 using crystal structures of a TCR–pMHC complex. The key finding was that the suboptimal MHC anchor residue in the natural peptide allows the TCR to mechanically "pull" the peptide away from the binding groove, creating additional TCR contact points with both peptide and MHC that are absent in the higher-affinity heteroclitic variant. This structural mechanism explains a critical problem in cancer immunotherapy: T cells primed with the heteroclitic peptide fail to efficiently recognize the natural tumor-expressed antigen, potentially accounting for clinical trial failures. The work demonstrates that while heteroclitic peptides improve MHC binding and elicit stronger initial immune responses, they may paradoxically reduce cross-reactivity to the native epitope by inducing a conformational state that does not match the TCR-engaged geometry of the wild-type peptide. These findings highlight the extreme conformational sensitivity of TCR recognition and the complex trade-offs between enhancing MHC stability and maintaining tumor-relevant antigen recognition in peptide-based cancer vaccines.

publications.title: TCR-induced alteration of primary MHC peptide anchor residue.
publications.authors: Madura F, Rizkallah PJ, Legut M, Holland CJ, Fuller A, Bulek A, Schauenburg AJ, Trimby A, Hopkins JR, Wells SA, Godkin A, Miles JJ, Sami M, Li Y, Liddy N, Jakobsen BK, Loveridge EJ, Cole DK, Sewell AK.
publications.executive_summary: The MEL5 T-cell receptor recognizes the nonapeptide AAGIGILTV (derived from the MART-1 melanoma tumor antigen) presented by HLA-A*02:01, a clinically relevant epitope that exists in both nona- and decapeptide forms at the tumor cell surface. Crystal or cryo-EM structural analysis revealed that TCR binding induces an altered conformation of the peptide's anchor residues at the MHC interface—a "flexing" mechanism that deviates from the canonical peptide-MHC binding mode. This induced-fit mechanism explains why the dominant nonapeptide form at melanoma surfaces is poorly recognized by most clinical TCRs despite being the naturally presented species, accounting for the disappointing clinical results of prior MART-1-targeted immunotherapies. The finding demonstrates that molecular plasticity of the peptide-MHC complex is a key determinant of TCR recognition and has direct implications for optimizing T-cell engineering and cancer immunotherapy design.

publications.title: Targeting of multiple tumor-associated antigens by individual T cell receptors during successful cancer immunotherapy.
publications.authors: Dolton G, Rius C, Wall A, Szomolay B, Bianchi V, Galloway SAE, Hasan MS, Morin T, Caillaud ME, Thomas HL, Theaker S, Tan LR, Fuller A, Topley K, Legut M, Attaf M, Hopkins JR, Behiry E, Zabkiewicz J, Alvares C, Lloyd A, Rogers A, Henley P, Fegan C, Ottmann O, Man S, Crowther MD, Donia M, Svane IM, Cole DK, Brown PE, Rizkallah P, Sewell AK.
publications.executive_summary: Individual TCRs isolated from melanoma patients undergoing successful TIL therapy exhibited remarkable polyfunctionality, each recognizing three distinct HLA-A*02:01-restricted tumor-associated peptides: EAAGIGILTV (Melan A), LLLGIGILVL (BST2), and NLSALGIFST (IMP2). Crystal structures of the TCR bound to all three epitopes revealed a shared x-x-x-A/G-I/L-G-I-x-x-x recognition motif that enables this multi-epitope targeting. The ability of individual TCRs to simultaneously attack cancer through multiple tumor-associated antigens confers superior recognition of cancer cells compared to conventional single-epitope TCR recognition. This multipronged targeting strategy is significant for cancer immunotherapy development, as it suggests that engineering or selecting T cells capable of recognizing multiple tumor antigens could enhance therapeutic efficacy and potentially reduce tumor escape through antigen loss.
